mnemonics: A Connected Space is Like a Single Piece: Think of a 'connected' space as one solid piece, not two separate chunks. Use the image of one unbroken cookie versus two separate cookies to remember that a disconnected space can be partitioned into two open, disjoint, non-empty sets.
keyPoints: A metric space is connected if it cannot be represented as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets.
Continuous images of connected sets are connected; this is a powerful tool for proving spaces are connected.
Intervals in the real line are connected; this is a fundamental result upon which many other connectedness proofs are built.
commonMistakes: Confusing a 'disconnected' space with one that is simply not simply connected; disconnected is a specific, stronger condition.
Assuming that if a set is not open, it must be disconnected; connectedness is independent of whether a set is open or closed.
Incorrectly applying the definition by using closed sets instead of open sets to test for connectedness; the formal definition requires two disjoint *open* sets.
